rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN MALLOY VS. CITY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Issuance Writ Of Mandate Per Ccp 1085, Ccp 1094.5, Ccp 425.10 Complaint; Ccp 527.6 Emotional Distress
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 19, 2025, line 1. PETITIONER STEPHEN MALLOY'S Motion For Issuance Writ Of Mandate Per CCP 1085, CCP 1094.5, CCP 425.10 Complaint; CCP 527.6 Emotional Distress. Plaintiff's petition for writ of mandate is denied. In violation of California Rules of Court Rule 3.1112(a)(3), plaintiff fails to provide a memorandum of points and authorities that explains why he is entitled to the requested relief. The record demonstrates that: (1) plaintiff made a broad request for "Everything of Public Record concerning Stephen Malloy in any Department of Public Health Department"; (2) defendant sought clarification regarding the request; (3) plaintiff failed to respond; and (4) defendant closed the request. (Lim Decl., Ex. A.) Petitioner fails to demonstrate an abuse of discretion or entitlement to traditional or administrative mandate. "Unquestionably, public records must be described clearly enough to permit the agency to determine whether writings of the type described in the request are under its control." (California First Amendment Coalition v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 159, 165.) "Petitioner should have presented a specific and focused request to the City, with which it then would have an opportunity to comply. It makes no sense to permit an individual to make a general, unfocused request for records to the public agency which will then be compelled to deny it, thereby ensuring litigation. The request to the agency must itself be focused and specific." (Rogers v. Superior Court (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 469, 481.) Defendant correctly notes that there is no pending records request and at this point, plaintiff should submit a new and more focused request. The court further notes that plaintiff does not explain a basis to recover damages. "[M]andamus is generally said not to be an appropriate remedy for recovering money." (Wenzler v. Municipal Court of Pasadena Judicial Dist. (1965) 235 Cal.App.2d 128, 133.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: MARY FELLER ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 19, 2025, line 4. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant City & County of San Francisco moves for summary judgment on Mary Feller's claim that a sidewalk defect was a dangerous condition of public property and on Gordon Feller's claim for loss of consortium. The court denies the motion. The court does not rule on the evidentiary objections because the cited evidence is unnecessary to disposition of this motion. (CCP 437c(q).) The tentative ruling in its entirety has been emailed to counsel. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. rulings.Case Title: AVONTE DARIUS CAMPINHA-BACOTE VS. TESLA, INC. ET AL
